cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the answer to this question. So the story is about an employer who's settling accounts with his children's governess, Julia Vassilyevna. The main character starts by saying they agreed on thirty rubles a month, but Julia had said forty. He then goes on to deduct various amounts for Sundays, holidays, days the kids were sick, her having a toothache, breaking a teacup, the kid climbing a tree and tearing his jacket, the maid stealing shoes, and some other payments. He keeps subtracting these amounts to try to calculate how much she owes, ending up with eleven rubles. But then he gets angry because she thanks him, realizing she's been cheated. Finally, he gives her all eighty rubles. The question is asking why the employer made all those criticisms about Julia's work. The options are: A) she had neglected her duties. B) he wanted to pay her as little money as possible. C) he wanted to make her realize that she was being cheated and protest against it. D) he wanted to make her feel miserable. Let me break it
